agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics system with established carrier assignment rules is being asked to add an auto-split feature that would break shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to fulfill a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The act of binding a single Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, it is locked and only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does not exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"A proposed automatic process that decomposes a Shipment exceeding a weight threshold into multiple Sub-Shipments, each with its own Carrier, without human authorization."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a structural integrity constraint. Downstream processes (tracking, billing, liability, proof of delivery) all assume a 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier relationship. Violating this corrupts the system's data model.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-draft state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting against unauthorized reassignment, which has liability, insurance, and chain-of-custody implications. Removing the Supervisor gate breaks the trust and audit model.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event must be associated with a Supervisor identity. Events without Supervisor authorization must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the dispatch-readiness invariant. A Shipment cannot leave without a single accountable Carrier. This is a physical and legal reality — someone must be responsible for the freight in transit.","testable_assertion":"No Shipment may transition to 'dispatched' status unless it has exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment of exactly 500 kg — does it split or not? Boundary ambiguity.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until the contradiction is resolved and the threshold rule is properly specified (strictly greater than vs. greater than or equal to)."},{"scenario":"A Shipment is assigned to a Carrier, then its weight is updated to exceed 500 kg after assignment.","expected_behavior":"Unclear. Auto-splitting a locked assignment would violate BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). The feature request does not address post-assignment weight changes."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting (e.g., a single indivisible item weighing 600 kg).","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting is impossible for indivisible cargo. The feature request does not account for item-level weight constraints."},{"scenario":"Liability dispute: cargo is damaged on a sub-shipment. Who is accountable — the original Shipment's Carrier or the sub-shipment's Carrier?","expected_behavior":"Cannot determine. The existing model assumes one Carrier bears full liability per Shipment. Splitting fractures the liability chain."},{"scenario":"Customer invoicing: the customer booked one Shipment. After auto-split, are they billed for multiple shipments?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The billing, pricing, and customer communication model is not addressed in the feature request."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments across carriers changes the pricing model, introduces multi-carrier coordination costs, and may trigger different rate tiers. Insurance and liability coverage must be renegotiated per sub-shipment.","operational":"Significant complexity increase. Dispatch, tracking, proof of delivery, and exception handling all assume one Carrier per Shipment. Every downstream system must be audited and potentially redesigned.","user":"Customers expect to track one shipment. Splitting into sub-shipments without customer awareness creates confusion. Supervisors lose oversight authority, undermining their operational role.","risk":"Critical. The feature as specified directly violates two production invariants (BR-1 in spirit by creating a multi-carrier model, and BR-2 by bypassing Supervisor authorization). Shipping without resolving these contradictions risks data corruption, liability gaps, and audit failures.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed. This undermines their operational control and may violate labor agreements or compliance policies."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"They may receive sub-shipments without the full context of the original shipment. SLA obligations, pickup coordination, and liability boundaries become ambiguous."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single booking may silently become multiple shipments. Tracking, delivery expectations, and billing change without their consent or awareness."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Revenue recognition, invoicing, and cost allocation models assume 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier. Auto-split breaks this assumption."},{"group":"Compliance / Legal","impact":"Chain-of-custody documentation, insurance coverage, and regulatory filings assume a single carrier per shipment. Splitting creates liability gaps."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Every system consuming Shipment events (tracking, notifications, billing, analytics) must be updated to handle the parent/child relationship. This is not a simple feature — it is a domain model evolution."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The specification contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants. The auto-split feature as described cannot coexist with the current domain model without either (a) redefining what a Shipment is, (b) introducing a new Sub-Shipment aggregate, or (c) relaxing the existing invariants. The feature request provides no guidance on which path to take, leaving fundamental domain questions unresolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant is clear and unambiguous","The bypass of Supervisor authority directly violates BR-2","Downstream systems (billing, tracking, liability) will be affected"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to introduce a new Sub-Shipment concept or modify the existing Shipment concept","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard business rule or a configurable policy","How liability, billing, and tracking should work across split shipments","Whether Supervisor bypass is intentional policy change or an oversight in the request"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are currently enforced in production and relied upon by downstream systems","The product team has not yet consulted with Legal, Compliance, or Finance on the implications of multi-carrier shipments"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment) and BR-2 (Supervisor-only reassignment). A Shipment that is split across multiple carriers either violates the one-Carrier invariant or requires introducing an entirely new domain concept (Sub-Shipment / Shipment Group) that does not exist in the current model. Additionally, auto-assigning carriers without Supervisor involvement violates the locked-assignment rule.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Compliance","Engineering"],"options":["OPTION A: Introduce a new 'Shipment Group' aggregate. The original Shipment becomes a parent, and Sub-Shipments are created as first-class Shipments, each with exactly one Carrier. The parent Shipment is never dispatched — only its children are. Supervisor approval is required to authorize the split.","OPTION B: Introduce a new 'Shipment Group' aggregate as in Option A, but allow auto-split without Supervisor approval — explicitly relaxing BR-2 for system-initiated splits only. This requires a formal policy change and audit trail.","OPTION C: Reject the feature as specified. Instead, flag shipments over 500 kg for Supervisor review, and the Supervisor manually creates the split, preserving all existing invariants."],"recommendation":"OPTION C in the short term (flag for Supervisor review, preserve invariants). If the business case demands full automation, pursue OPTION A with Supervisor approval as a gate. OPTION B should only be considered after Legal and Compliance review. Under no circumstances should the existing invariants be silently violated."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what was a single Shipment without introducing a new domain concept to preserve the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor authorization for carrier assignment, which is a locked operation in the current domain model.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."}]}
